sentences.text: Oh, I, I, I'm not sure he was captain or a lieutenant.
I-- his name is Dr.
Kimmerling.
And he was from, from Lviv.
And he took care of me and my brother.
And I have to be grateful to him for my health today.
Because I was needing that help, that convalescent.
Anyhow, I was, I was-- the convalescent was very good.
Because we had a lot of food and a variety of food.
And the nurses were taking us to the ocean, specially me.
I gained weight.
But they had to lift me up on a truck.
I couldn't-- I didn't have any muscle tone.
I couldn't lift myself up.
Although I gained already weight, but I didn't have the strength to get on a truck.
And they had to lift me up.
And they took us to the ocean.
And it, it was very nice.
I'm very grateful that they-- I had that opportunity.
Anyhow, this was-- and then after that four, five weeks, I think, we go back to our unit and started to exercise and play soccer and, and [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] beginning to feel like a human being.
And one of those days, one of those nights, I had a dream and woke up in the morning and told about the dream to my brother, Sam.
And I said, Sam-- we had relatives in Detroit in the United States.
Let's write to them.
And Sam sat down right away, wrote, wrote to the forward, I think.
And, a few days later came a reply.
Yes, they are, that they are our cousins-- I mean, my mother's cousins.
And they send us a picture of our parents.
And right away, things started to roll for us.
After, after the war, after when the war ended they published a, a list with the survivors.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We didn't know anything, me and Sam, that we didn't read.
Because we didn't stay in Germany.
The people who stayed in Germany and Austria knew about those papers.
Because we got to Italy, we were not in, in, in contact with any of the refugees or anybody, where the bulk was in Germany.
So Bernard was looking at those lists of survivors.
And he found us.
And he followed us.
When he found us, he was step by step following.
And he came to Italy.
And he found us in Italy.
That's how Bernard found us.
We didn't find Bernard.
We didn't know nothing.
So Bernard found us.
Bernard is my youngest brother.
So he found us in Italy.
We already enrolled in the army.
And he was staying at that place, Santa Maria di Bagni.
This is very southern Italy.
He was staying in a, in a DP camp.
And we were in the army already at a place called Barletta.
This is, I think, 12 kilometers from this, from Bari that's on the Adriatic Ocean.
What were you doing in the army?
Were you training?
What--
Beside doing the regular training, what soldiers do, we were also holding a-- we were guard unit.
We were doing the guard unit for, for supply-- food supplies-- and also, also civilian, civilian displaced person, Polish displaced person.
We were, we were guarding them, and also some food depots.
And we were holding the, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the-- on the ocean.
What you call it?
The, the, the-- The docks?
The docks, yes, guarding the docks and stuff like this.
We were doing a lot of guard duty, guard duty.
And this was in Italy.
I stayed in Italy with the Polish army-- oh, then, then, then we met a few Jewish.

Oh, then we-- in September, I think, 1945, we had the gathering of the-- for the high holidays, all the servicemen from all the-- from Britain, from, from India, from, from the Polish armies, from all the Allied Air Force were gathering at a place in Bari for the high holidays.
They-- it was a place run by the British called NAAFI.
NAAFI was like the American-- what is it that they-- where they do the entertainment?
The--
The SO, the--
USO, yeah, something like this.
So this was-- they were running by the British.
And we met a lot of, lot of people, a lot of people there from, from, from-- all the, all Jews from all over the world, gathering for the high holidays, for Rosh Hashanah, Yom Kippur.
And I'm standing on the corner in Bari.
And all of a sudden, I looked up.
A guy in a, in a uniform, in a, in a British uniform with a Polish eagle on top on the beret, my mother's cousin, Ignatz!
I said, what are you doing here?
He asked me the same question.
What you doing here?
He was the last fellow that I saw in 1939 being in the Polish army.
He went through Russia, drafted in the Polish army, through the Middle East into Italy, into Montecasino.
And now he was in, in, in the Occupation Force in Italy.
And that's where I met him, on the corner in Bari.
Yes.
Not official, not official, not official-- it was prohibited because it was under British command.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was, I think, the Third Army.
It was under General Montgomery who was at the head of the, at the head of the army.
Anyhow--
Oh, we stayed in Italy till winter '45.
Beginning '46, we were-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] oh, before I go to that, I-- so we were-- we could take our families to England.
This was our ticket to England.
While we were in, in, in the-- visiting Bernard, we met somebody down there, a, a woman by the name of [?
Hila ?] Singer.
This was her married name.
I, I don't remember her single name.
And she told us that she has family in England, that she is willing to go.
She, she will go to England eventually.
So we said, we can take you.
Because we can take family.
But the deal is-- but you might go before us.
The civilian might be shipped out before us, providing that you take care of Bernard.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So she had to change her name.
She went under our name.
She was converted into orphan.
And Bernard went with her to England.
So Bernard went few months before us.
He had a home already, where to go.
So Bernard wind up in England before us.
Then we were in, in January, I think, '46, I think, we were shipped out to England, into Scotland, the border Scotland and-- Scotland and England.
Oh, was snowdrifts were, were 20 feet maybe.
It was a terrible disaster down there in Scotland at that time.
Thousands of cattles were dying in the snowdrift, and anyhow.
Anyhow, we got down there.
It was cold.
It was, it was terrible.
We lived on those metal, metal barracks, which was freezing, freezing down there.
Anyhow we met some, some-- a cup of Jews, though, though was a place called Carlisle in Cumberland.
This was in Scottish border.
And we couldn't speak English.
And they couldn't speak, they couldn't speak Yiddish.
And we couldn't communicate.
There was, there was a disaster.
And I knew that I get myself in trouble.
If I refuse a cup of tea to an Englishman, I am in trouble.
And we were making a motion with our hands.
I don't know what was the conversation going on, how bad.
I was drinking about four cups of tea.
And I said, hey, this is the limit already.
And I said, hey, listen.
But I knew.
I was told that you can cannot refuse an Englishman a cup of tea.
That is an insult.
Anyhow, end of the story-- to make the end of the story, I turned the cup upside down.
I said, that's it, you know?
[LAUGHS] It was-- And they understood.
Was--
No.
Then, then we got to England.
Then our cousins from Detroit send us a telegram that we want to come to visit us to England.
OK.
This was already summer, I think, '46, summer '46.
And they are coming.
I don't know.
We got ourself in trouble.
I don't want to go into details.
We got ourself in trouble.
And we took a leave without absent.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we were, we were sitting in, in the-- what you called it?
Not jail-- what you call it the military, in the-- In the Navy, it's the brig.
But I guess it's the army's equivalent of a brig.
In the brigs, OK.
There you go.
OK.
We were sitting in the brig.
We ourself in trouble without leave.
Anyhow, and there, our cousins were coming from, from America.
We wanted leave.
And they wouldn't give us.
Eh.
We went on a hunger strike.
And we didn't accept any food.
Anyhow, make the story short, finally, we [INAUDIBLE].
They let us out.
We got to London.
And we got our, we got our leave.
And my god, they brought food.
They brought a salami like this.
And I never saw in my life, and bread.
They brought food from America.
And we were staying in London in a hotel.
It was nice.
Anyhow, then they got to know some people.
We couldn't get discharged from the army if we didn't have a job.
So we got-- from the army, we, we couldn't get discharged.
Because you had to get-- have a job already.
So they discharge you from the army.
So they got familiar with some people in London.
And we got some papers.
And in the meantime, we were attending, attending dances down there in, in, in Carlisle in the NAAFI, the NAAFI, like I said before, the military-- the British equivalent to the USO.
And a fellow came over to us.
And he said, you speak Yiddish?
I said, yes.
He was from London.
And he, he wanted to get into the dance hall.
But we could bring one civilian into the dance hall.
We were entitled to bring in.
So he wanted.
So we took him in.
And we started to talk.
He told us about he has an aunt in London.
And he's from London and, and so forth.
And we told him that we can get jobs, but we got no place where to live.
So he says with his aunt, you could, you could make some kind of a deal.
You could get rooms with her and board.
Anyhow, so, so we knew already somebody in London.
And we got some papers to get out of it-- get the job.
We got discharged from the army.
I came to the-- to Mrs.
Friedman was her name.
Yes, she was very nice.
She was-- we lived with it for a few years, for five, six years.
She was like a mother to us.
She was so good.
The three brothers lived with her afterwards.
And--
And then, then I went to night school.
Because I got tired of walking around with a piece of paper and asking people for direction.
And everybody thinks that you are dumb.
They were pointing to this direction, that direction.
And it was-- I went to night school to learn English.
And and there was a shortage of books.
Was on the east side.
There was a shortage of books.
So the teacher assigned me with another young Polish girl to sit with her and share, share.
And her name was Helen Goldberg.
And eventually, I married her.
Well Helen was a very shy girl.
She was very shy.
I done most of the talking.
She was nine years younger than I.
She was blonde, had a beautiful figure, good looking.
I fell in love with her.
But first, I ignored it.
Because she was too young for me.
I-- she was too young for me.
So I didn't.
But then eventually, we found things in common.
And I brought her over to my club, introduced her to some fellows in my neighborhood.
I belonged to a young, young, young men's club.
And we start going out.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And that's what happened, going out.
I didn't marry Helen till 1951, till I came over to this country.
We got engaged in, in England.
Mrs.
Friedman made us a big, big engagement party.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And--
Oh, Helen-- yeah, that's a very interesting story how Helen came.
Helen was-- Helen was an orphan brought up by her grandmother.
We, we never know the story behind what actually happened.
But she ran out.
She ran away from the ghetto.
She was about--
Dombrowa, near Tarnow.
She ran away from the ghetto.
She was, she was maybe seven years old.
It was in winter.
She ran out of the ghetto.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] she said she slipped out between the legs of a big guy, of a big SS man.
And she ran into the neighborhood farms and so forth.
One-- she didn't have shoes.
This was in winter.
And one of the guys said, I'm going to call the German.
You are Jew.
I'm going to call the Germans on you.
You better get out of here.
Then another place, they chased her.
And finally, she got-- at night, she got exhausted.
She got to one of the stables into a farmer.
And she fell asleep in, in the stables.
In midnight, I guess, the farmer came in to check on his stock.
Usually they come in.
And he found her there, laying in the, in the hay.
And he didn't say a word.
He picked her up.
He took it into the-- to his, to his house, sat her on the stove, gave her a glass of milk.
And, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and he says, you can stay here.
He said, you're going to take care of my wife.
His wife was dying of-- I don't know-- cancer or what.
So she was taking care of his wife and helping and taking care of the cattle [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and working on the farm.
And he was telling everybody that this is a niece of his, that she came from, from Poznan, from some, some other, other area of the-- a refugee.
And Helen went-- she was blonde and light skin and blue eyes.
And she went to learn a little bit about the church.
She went to church and sang Christmas carols.
And, and there was one of the-- of the sons was a tailor.
And the Germans used to come to have their uniforms fixed.
And, and this was destroyed.
That's how she lived through-- the war ended already.
I'm going-- the war ended already.
It was a year maybe after the war or so.
And she had a dream.
She told-- she's telling me that.
Her mother came through and told her that she should go to the village.
She knew that the war was over, because the Russians were there already.
And she should go to the village and find out what's cooking.
So she goes to the village.
She found one of her cousins.
And he told her that there is a doctor a rabbi from England, taking all the orphans kids to London, that she could go with him.
Yes.
So-- where was I?
Oh, oh, so she went to, to-- so she went-- so she wind up in England.
So she wind up in England.
And that's where I met her, in-- and she had, she had cousins in England.
She, she had family in England, aunts and uncles in England, Helen.
So she stayed with them.
And she went to school at night.
That's how I met her.
And that's the story with--
And, and, and-- OK, so let me come to it.
So in England-- then, then in 1951, I came over first.
I came in on a special act of Congress.
They gave to all the, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] all the soldiers, Allied soldiers, they give-- what you call it-- visas to come.
Anyhow, so I, I came to the United States.
We had-- we got the affidavits from our relatives, and anyhow.
Anyhow, and Helen came.
We got engaged in London, like I said before, before she left.
And she came a couple of months later.
Her uncle-- she had an uncle in Jersey City who send her a visa.
And she came here a couple months later.
And we got married in June the 15, 1951, in Jersey City.
In Jersey City, we got together.
I, I came together with my brothers in 1951.
The three of us came together to Detroit.
Well, I worked, I worked in the, in the, in the fashion industry.
I was a grade A pattern maker.
And I'd done everything-- cutting.
I done everything in the fashion industry, whatever, whatever had to be done.
And then, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] then Helen, then we had-- I-- then we had our oldest daughter, Ruth, 1952.
Ruth was born 1952.
I was-- I was afraid to touch her.
On the beginning, I had a guilt complex.
Do I deserve to have a child or anything?
I had a guilt complex.
And then-- you see, I first was in Detroit.
But then I came here.
And I was in Jersey City.
I said Helen doesn't have anybody, any family or anything.
So I will stay here and try to make a living here.
And my brothers were in Detroit.
And I was very close with my brother.
And I was very lonely not to have anybody here, not to have family.
I was looking over for somebody to give me some little advice or whatever.
I was needing somebody.
I, I didn't have any.
Anyhow, so then we had Ruth.
And we lived in Jersey City on Magnolia Avenue, five, five flights walk up, dumbwaiter with the cockroaches coming up with the dumbwaiter.
And anyhow, was hot in summer like hell.
But the, the beauty about it was on the fifth floor, it was a corner apartment.
And the view was magnificent.
We saw the Hudson River with the big boats coming in, with the Queens, with everything.
And I enjoyed this immensely.
I enjoyed that.
That view was out of this world.
I was lonely.
But somehow, when I looked out, I saw the people traveling and everything.
I was happy.
But I, I got-- my first job was in Red Bank.
I didn't know where was the garment industry or anything.
I took a job in Red Bank.
I had to catch the train from Jersey City, go to Red Bank.
This was-- people hated me down there, because I took away the job from the farmers down-- there was all farms down there.
It's not build up like today.
So eventually, I found out where was the garment center.
Yes, when I was hired down there in, in-- it was the plant.
What was the plant's name?
Oh, was a big military plant, making uniforms for the army.
They asked me, did I work in this country?
I said, no.
This will be my first job.
So he took me to that plant.
It was tremendous size of a plant.
He asked me, could you tell me?
Did you see equipment like this?
I said, yes, everything.
But I said, I did-- we didn't have cogs machines in the plant.
I said, this is the only.
But otherwise, the equipment, everything is the same.
And he asked me, how much did you make in England?
I said, I said about [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] 15 pounds, I think. 15 pounds, I think.
How much would that be American money?
I bet $45.
He said, you, you, you hired.
By the time I paid for my first and my everything, I didn't anything.
I had to pay my rent.
I didn't have any money left.
Anyhow, anyhow, then we had Ruth.
And Ruth, then Gail was born three years, then Suzanne-- three daughters apart-- three daughters three years apart practically.
And then [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Helene one day took her high school equivalency test.
She learned a lot from the kids.
Then she went, then she went to high school, passed the high school equivalency test.
Then she went to college, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] graduated college.
Then she went to interior, interior decorating school, passed that, and opened up a business, became interior decorator.
And things beginning to get a little better for us.
She could only go to school.
My kids never had keys to the house.
She could go only to school at night when I came home from work and in the daytime up till 3:00.
I didn't care what she'd done in the house.
It didn't bother me.
But as long as the-- when she came home-- she had to be home when the kids came home 3:00.
So she took courses.
She schedule everything her life.
And she was very bright, bright woman, very bright woman.
She accomplished a lot.
Thank you.
We'll pause here to change tapes.
